name: Long paper - caching demo (normal)
markdown:
# Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Practical Overview

## Introduction

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) couples a language model with an external memory so that
answers are grounded in source documents rather than the model's parametric knowledge alone.
The motivation is twofold: factual grounding and freshness. A model cannot recall a document it
never saw during training, and it cannot be trusted to reproduce specific figures verbatim. By
retrieving relevant passages at query time and conditioning the generation on them, a RAG system
can cite its sources and stay current as the corpus grows. This document surveys the moving parts
of a modern pipeline and the trade-offs that govern each choice.

## Parsing

Before anything can be retrieved it must be read. Parsing converts a heterogeneous corpus of PDFs,
scans, and office files into clean text. The hard cases are mathematical notation, tables, multi
column layouts, and broken font encodings that produce garbled character streams. A hosted vision
model that transcribes a rendered page image handles these uniformly, at the cost of a model call
per page. Cheaper paths, such as reading an embedded text layer, work only when that layer is clean.
A good parser routes each page to the cheapest safe method and preserves the page number so that any
downstream answer can cite an exact location in the original document.

## Chunking

Parsed text is too long to embed as a single unit, so it is split into chunks. The state of the art
is unglamorous: cut on real structure, such as headings and paragraphs, and bound each chunk to a few
hundred tokens. Elaborate semantic splitters that place boundaries by embedding similarity are slower
and rarely better. The cut itself is low leverage. What matters is that each chunk carries provenance,
namely the document, the page, and the section it came from, and that boundaries do not slice a formula
or a table in half. A small overlap between neighbours protects a sentence that straddles a boundary.

## Contextual retrieval

A chunk pulled out of its document loses context. A paragraph that says the method improves results by
ten percent is useless if the reader cannot tell which method or which results. Contextual retrieval
fixes this by asking a language model to write a short blurb situating each chunk within its parent
document, and prepending that blurb before the chunk is embedded and indexed. The reported effect is a
large reduction in retrieval failures. The cost is one model call per chunk, but because the document is
sent as a stable prefix on every call, prompt caching serves that prefix cheaply after the first chunk.

## Hybrid search and reranking

Dense vector search captures meaning but misses exact terms; lexical search captures exact terms but
misses paraphrase. Fusing the two recovers both. A cross encoder then reranks the fused candidates,
reading each query and passage together rather than comparing fixed vectors. Each layer compounds the
previous one, and the combination substantially outperforms vectors alone on realistic queries.

## The graph layer

Some questions cannot be answered by any single passage because they require connecting facts stated in
different places. A graph layer extracts entities and relationships from the chunks and links them, so a
query can traverse from one fact to a related one several hops away. Independent evaluation suggests the
graph adds a modest but real gain over a strong vector base, and only on genuinely multi hop questions,
so it is built after the base is proven and measured, not before.

## Conclusion

A retrieval pipeline is a sequence of independent, swappable steps. Each step has a cheap default and an
expensive upgrade, and the right design measures before it spends. Parsing, chunking, contextualization,
hybrid search, reranking, and an optional graph layer together turn a static pile of documents into a
memory a model can cite.


## Embedding models

The embedding model decides what "similar" means. For a multilingual or technical corpus the choice
matters more than the vector store, because a model that was trained mostly on English web text will
place a French scientific paragraph far from the English query that should match it. Benchmarks like
MTEB give a starting ranking, but they are reported on public datasets and rarely predict performance
on a private, domain specific corpus. The pragmatic rule is to keep the embedder swappable behind a
single interface and to validate the top few candidates on a held out set of real questions before
committing. Dimensionality, context length, and licensing are secondary considerations that only
matter once two models are otherwise close on accuracy.

## Evolutive memory

A corpus is not static. New documents arrive, facts change, and old claims are superseded. A naive
system rebuilds its index from scratch on every change, which is wasteful and loses history. A better
design treats memory as append only at its core, recording every ingested fact with the time it was
seen, and derives the searchable index from that log. When a fact changes, the old edge is marked
invalid rather than deleted, so the system can still answer questions about what was believed at an
earlier date. A background process consolidates duplicates and reconciles conflicts, but it never
edits the log in place. Crucially, each consolidation pass is treated as a candidate that is promoted
only if an evaluation shows it improves answers, because unchecked self rewriting has been shown to
degrade memory below the level of having no memory at all.

## Evaluation

None of these choices can be made by intuition. Every claim that a layer helps must be checked on a
representative set of questions drawn from the actual corpus, scored against known answers. The
evaluation set is the single most valuable artifact in the project, because it converts every design
debate into a measurement. Without it, teams add complexity that feels sophisticated but quietly makes
retrieval worse, and they have no way to notice. With it, the graph layer, the reranker, and the
contextualizer each earn their place or are removed.

## Deployment constraints

Finally, the environment shapes the architecture. A system that will run on locked down corporate
machines cannot depend on heavy local models, a private graph database server, or unrestricted package
installation. It can only assume a hosted model endpoint and a small set of pure python libraries.
This constraint is not a limitation to be lamented but a design input to be respected from the first
line of code, because retrofitting a pipeline that assumed a powerful workstation onto a restricted
laptop is far more expensive than designing for the laptop from the start.

items.context: This chunk discusses the dynamic nature of a corpus in a retrieval-augmented generation system, emphasizing the importance of maintaining an append-only memory design to efficiently handle updates, preserve historical facts, and ensure the accuracy of answers over time. It is part of the section on "Evolutive memory," which outlines strategies for managing changes in the document corpus without losing valuable information.
